lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Threescore and ten I can remember well:

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Within the volume of which time I have seen

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Hours dreadful and things strange; but this sore night

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Hath trifled former knowings.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Ah, good father,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Thou seest, the heavens, as troubled with man’s act,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Threaten his bloody stage: by the clock, ’tis day,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: And yet dark night strangles the travelling lamp:

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Is’t night’s predominance, or the day’s shame,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: That darkness does the face of earth entomb,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: When living light should kiss it?

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: ’Tis unnatural,

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Even like the deed that’s done. On Tuesday last,

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: A falcon, towering in her pride of place,

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: Was by a mousing owl hawk’d at and kill’d.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: And Duncan’s horses—a thing most strange and certain—

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Beauteous and swift, the minions of their race,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Turn’d wild in nature, broke their stalls, flung out,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Contending ’gainst obedience, as they would make

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: War with mankind.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: ’Tis said they eat each other.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: They did so, to the amazement of mine eyes

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: That look’d upon’t. Here comes the good Macduff.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Why, see you not?

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Is’t known who did this more than bloody deed?

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Those that Macbeth hath slain.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Alas, the day!

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: What good could they pretend?

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: They were suborn’d:

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Malcolm and Donalbain, the king’s two sons,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Are stol’n away and fled; which puts upon them

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Suspicion of the deed.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: ’Gainst nature still!

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Thriftless ambition, that wilt ravin up

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Thine own life’s means! Then ’tis most like

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: The sovereignty will fall upon Macbeth.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: He is already named, and gone to Scone

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: To be invested.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Where is Duncan’s body?

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Carried to Colmekill,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: The sacred storehouse of his predecessors,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: And guardian of their bones.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Will you to Scone?

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: No, cousin, I’ll to Fife.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Well, I will thither.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Well, may you see things well done there: adieu!

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Lest our old robes sit easier than our new!

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Farewell, father.

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: God’s benison go with you; and with those

lines.speaker: OLD MAN
lines.text: That would make good of bad, and friends of foes!

stage_directions.text: Enter Ross and an old Man.
How goes the world, sir, now?

glosses.definition: sweep of history I've lived through
made what I knew before seem trivial
the sun, figured as a traveling lantern

glosses.phrase: towering in her pride of place
glosses.definition: at the peak of her flight — a falcon image
an owl that normally hunts mice — far below a falcon in the natural order
the finest of their breed — Duncan's horses

glosses.phrase: Turn’d wild in nature, broke their stalls
glosses.definition: went mad, broke out of their stables
Duncan's horses ate each other — the most monstrous of the portents
the traditional site of Scottish coronations
a region of eastern Scotland; Macduff's home

annotations.title: Nature's inversions
annotations.body:
The scene outside Macbeth's castle catalogs a series of impossibilities. The sun has not risen though it is day. A falcon has been killed by a mouse-eating owl ('towering in her pride of place'). Duncan's horses have broken their stalls and eaten each other. None of these is decorative; each is the natural order registering, in its own dialect, that the king has been murdered.

The Tudor and Jacobean theory of correspondence — the doctrine that the political order mirrors the natural order, and that regicide disrupts both — is given its clearest poetic expression in this scene. The mousing owl killing the falcon is a picture of a tyrant killing a king; the horses eating each other is the state turning on itself; the darkness at noon is the sun's refusal to light a disordered world.

Shakespeare is not being superstitious here; he is showing us the vocabulary in which his audience would have processed political crisis. When, forty lines later, Ross tells us 'Adieu, / Lest our old robes sit easier than our new,' the clothing-image returns: the state is dressed in the wrong clothes, and until the right king is found, it will not fit.
sources: E. M. W. Tillyard, *The Elizabethan World Picture* (1943)

trials.prompt: The portents Ross reports (sun dark at noon, falcon killed by an owl, Duncan's horses eating each other) function as:
options: The natural order's registration of regicide — correspondence theory put into stage imagery

trials.prompt: Who is named as the new king?
options: Macbeth — he will be crowned at Scone
